channel.title: Feature Requests (new threads)
channel.description: Threads in the forum category "Feature Requests" - What features or capabilities would you like to see in the next version of the Foundation website?

item.title: Advanced Rank System
item.content:encoded:
Description: Replace Karma with a rank system that reflects your function on the wiki. The system would be managed by administrators and would allow for the creation of custom ranks.

Reason: The karma system is quite useless and random, I think that a system that would allow to know what are the prerogatives of a person we talk to on the site (thanks to an icon for example) would be more useful.

Advantages: Know a user/staff member's function more clearly.

Disadvantages: For the moment I do not find any disadvantages.

item.title: Better curation/discovery tools
item.content:encoded:
Description: New or expanded ways to curate and find content. I don't have a specific suggestion just now, but I know there have been some threads over the years (e.g., in proposals and policy) where recommendations have just fallen through because wikidot couldn't handle them.

This might involve looking at other communities and organizations for curation methods, making it easy for readers to categorize or reccomend articles, or some kind of more complex technical approach.

(Side thought, it would also help to interview some representative users to identify their needs and current search techniques so that you could build on those.)

At the very least, you could add code features wikidot doesn't have so that people can add these later.

Reason: Wikiwalking and finding good content is the core activity for readers, and for some articles like tales discoverability is seriously lacking.

Advantages: ^

Disadvantages: I guess it would take some effort to identify and implement the best techniques.

item.title: Scpper-like features
item.content:encoded:
Description: Scpper-like stats & features built into the wiki.

Reason: Scpper provides a ton of useful features (searchable by keyword or author, tracks stats over time, archives deleted articles) for both tracking individual articles and wiki-wide functionally.

Advantages: Makes stats-nerds (users and staff) happy, helpful with discovery.

Also (and I mean absolutely no shade to FiftyNine here, they are wonderful), integrating it and hosting it on the same servers improves access to the tools and could lead to less chug.

Disadvantages: Possibly difficult integration, don't want to steal FiftyNine's thunder.

item.title: Word Count
item.description: Writing concepts, or maybe a draft. Might be nice to have word count
item.content:encoded:
Description: Pretty simple idea, simply add a word count box below text boxes for purposes of knowing. Highlighting certain parts of text gives you word count for that part.

Reason: Quite a dumb one really, but when revising my concept, I keep forgetting to check word count. It would probably be helpful to just have a box for words. A small idea mainly for convenience, but a welcome one.

Advantages: No need to check word count using foreign website, ease of access and remembering.

Disadvantages: Not really, unless you wanna count the tiny bit of extra space it takes.

item.title: Retain the RSS feed system
item.content:encoded: I currently use the RSS Feeds associated with the "new posts" link on the discussion threads for my SCPs. It would be nice if these RSS Feeds were still available in the next version of the website.

item.title: Keeping the look of the website consistent
item.description: As of late SCP Foundation has been a bit inconsistent
item.content:encoded:
Recently, the SCP Foundation has changed the sites feel and look

Now, this would be fine if the changes had been throughout the site, but other parts of the site including the front page stick to the original feel, and there is even a third one, unrelated to the new one and old one.

All I ask is that once we move away from Wikidot that we try to keep the feel of the site consistent.

item.title: W3.CSS Integration
item.content:encoded:
Description:
Add W3.CSS or a better alternative to the wiki.
Reason:
Because this thing looks like it would make CSS styling of any kind about 15 times easier and more compatible cross-platform.

Also, I didn't see a feature request for it here and thought it would be too big of a thing to miss just because a request wasn't made lol
Advantages:
W3.CSS is designed to be a flexible, modern, responsive, and overall better Bootstrap alternative. It's built for compatibility with as many platforms as possible and (from what I have seen) has very little jank. Also, W3.CSS is free to use. No license is necessary.
Disadvantages:
I can't think of any downsides that aren't already in place because CSS can be used on the wiki. W3.CSS is entirely CSS-based (from what they're saying on their site), so no chance of any JavaScript being misused/getting in the way and no ability to execute arbitrary HTML with it.
Note:
Now, I haven't looked too deep into it, so I don't know if there's already a better alternative, but I feel like something to this effect would be an outstanding feature post-migration. Potentially even pre-migration, if that's viable, since it's a simple <link> to add it to a website.

item.title: Notification System Addition.
item.description: Be notified when someone replies to your posts.
item.content:encoded: Okay, so the other day, I was talking to one of the Butterfly Squad Roster members about a concept, and I was just thinking, "Hey, wouldn't it be easier if you had an option to be notified when someone replies to a post you made in a thread?" Like if you got a Wikidot notification when someone posted a reply to a thread you made, and had an option to disable it for certain threads, or entirely? I don't know. I'm new here. Maybe it'll be added. Maybe it won't. Just a little suggestion to help improve the site! Please PM me if you respond to this, as Wikidot won't notify me otherwise, which is what I'm trying to change! Have a nice day wherever you are!

item.title: HTML Blocks
item.description: But different than Wikidot has them
item.content:encoded:
Description: Basically, I'm suggesting a syntax/some other way to parse HTML as it's normally done. Not in <iframe> blocks like how it's done currently.

Reason: This would open up so many more new format screws, cool formatting in general, functionality, and literally everything for anyone who knows HTML.

Advantages: See the above. (More functionality and potential for cool shit)

Disadvantages: There is, unfortunately, the potential for misuse of any freedom like this. For example, malicious code that steals passwords, forced downloads, etc. I do think there's enough good policing of this community, however, that the good here outweighs the bad.

item.title: PF should use a more gradated rating system
item.content:encoded:
Description: I believe PF should have a rating module which offers 7 options as opposed to the current 2: -3, -2, -1, +0, +1, +2, and +3. When importing existing wiki content, I would treat all existing -1s and +1s as -2s and +2s respectively, and new user guides would emphasize that +2 and -2 are the "default" expression of approval/disapproval, with the 1s and 3s being more or less extreme versions. Deletions guidelines would be adjusted accordingly.

Reason: A -1/+1 binary is far too simple and uninformative for the work we do on the site. I cannot reflect the difference between a work I find to be truly exceptional and a work I only barely consider good in the work's rating, nor can I distinguish a poorly conceived coldpost and something with real potential that just falls short. Worst of all, I cannot tell an author that I read their work and consciously decided not to change the rating one way or another, except with a "novote" comment, which is an issue as I generally couldn't articulate why I decided to novote. As an author, I would love to know how many people no-voted on my own works.

Advantages: This would promote a more critical userbase. There are MANY times I've read an SCP that I consider to be competent but very mediocre. On some level I understand the wiki would be better off if works like these were not on it, but I simply cannot bring myself to downvote. It feels too rude to me to essentially say such a work should be booted off the wiki. However if I had a less severe version of a downvote (a -1 as opposed to the more standard -2), I know I would be more comfortable using it, and in doing so promote a higher standard of work. Another situation I often find myself in is reading an SCP I know is highly flawed but has a few elements that I find so interesting or compelling that I feel I must upvote it. As it stands, my +1 entirely cancels the -1 of a more critical site member. But under my proposed system, I would give it only a +1, as opposed to the more standard +2, in recognition of its faults. Now a more critical user's -2 or -3 can overtake my vote's influence. Based on certain recent discussions on the philosophy of novotes and downvotes I've seen, I know I'm not alone in voting in this fashion, I saw experienced, prolific authors express feelings much like my own.
This system would also help author's get a much better idea of how much their audience enjoyed the work, rather than a mere liked it/didn't like it binary.

Disadvantages: The intuitive sense for the "value" of a rating that site users have developed over the years will no longer be valid and will have to be adjusted. Treating the new +2 and -2 as being equivalent to the old +1 and -1 essentially halves the value of each rating point.
People may be inclined to treat +1 and -1 the same way they have been on wikidot, with +/-2 being an extreme version and +/-3 being an extra extreme version. This could perhaps be corrected by making only +/-2 and +0 available on the rating module by default, with the 1s and 3s available via a small pulldown menu, to indicate they should be used less often. It could also be corrected by making the new system -1.5, -1.0, -.5, +0, +.5, +1.0, and +1.5.
Users transferring to the new site would be unlikely to go through all their existing +/-1s (Now +/-2s) and adjust them according to the new system. Consequently, the rating system would not have quite the same meaning for works originally made on wikidot as it would for works made on the new site.

item.title: Edit buttons
item.description: In which the suite of buttons for editing wiki articles are discussed, and new ones proposed
item.content:encoded:
If someday the SCP wiki were to be its own thing rather than being tied to wikidot.com, would the new independent site be able to have its own edit buttons?

I'm talking about the series of buttons that are right above the forum text entry window that I'm typing into right now - which is the same set of buttons that appears above the window into which we type when creating or editing a wiki article.

"H1" "B" "I" "U" "S" and so on.

It strikes me that this collection of buttons may not correspond to the actually-most-useful formatting options that SCP community members frequently use.

If the collection of edit buttons were to be re-designed from the ground up (not that I have any idea whether this is possible or whether anyone would choose to take the project on), what would we want that collection of buttons to include?

Some potential suggestions:

  • A "SCP Mainlist" button which, with a single click, creates a template mainlist article (rating module, boldfaced "Item", "Object Class," "Special Containment Procedures," "Description," wikiwalk nav footer, and other popular footers)
  • A button that opens a wizard to create a collapsible
  • Buttons that format blocks of text in certain popular styles, such as various colored boxes, green-on-black old CRT style text, text that looks like a handwritten note, etc.
  • image wizard that actually facilitates the formatting of images in the way that the SCP wiki typically formats them
  • Button that opens up a menu of page styles
  • The greatest hits of http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-style-resource

item.title: How to use the Feature Requests forum
item.description: PLEASE READ THIS BEFORE POSTING IN THIS CATEGORY
item.content:encoded:
This board will be for suggestions and feature requests for Project Foundation. Please see the General Information board for more information on Project Foundation.

What is this forum?

Wikidot is alright, but it has its limits. If you've ever had a frustration with it — a bug, or a missing feature, or something else — you've just had to put up with it. However, at the conclusion of Project Foundation, we will have complete control over our platform. That means we'll be free to implement the changes that we want to — that you want to.

So, that's what this board is here for. Tell us what you want to change! If there's anything you ever wished you could change about the site but couldn't — and that includes both the SCP Wiki and Wikidot — here is the place to say it. Equally, if there's anything you like and definitely don't want to change, here is also the place to say.

A tiny suggestion is just as important as a big suggestion! (Also, tiny suggestions are way easier to implement.)

Please note that if your suggestion is possible to implement right now on Wikidot, your thread will be moved to Proposals and Policy where it can be discussed and implemented much sooner.

Rules

  • Post ONE idea per thread. If you have multiple ideas, make multiple threads.
    • If you also have a question, post the suggestion here, and ask the question in General Information.
  • Follow the template at the bottom of this post, if applicable.
  • Comment on other suggestions to show support for or debate an idea.
  • Try to avoid making duplicate suggestions. Prefer leaving a comment in the idea's thread to making a new one.

Caveats

  • Just because a feature is suggested, there is no guarantee it will make it into the final version of PF. Possible reasons a suggestion would not get implemented: technical limitations, financial limitations, contradiction to best practices, and philosophical differences, among other reasons.
  • If you suggest a feature, please explain why you believe the feature should be implemented. Note that "because I think it would be cool" is a valid reason for supporting a feature.
  • The rules of critiquing articles apply to critiquing suggestions posted here: criticise the idea, not the author; explain why you disagree with a suggestion, where possible; and be civil. But please do respond to ideas!
  • Please do not suggest any silly or patently absurd features. Suggestions that are wholly outside the realm of possibility will be considered spam and treated accordingly.
  • Just because an idea has already been suggested, that doesn't mean you can't suggest/support the idea also. If possible please do leave a comment in that idea's thread to show your support — that helps us gauge support for any given suggestion.

Suggestions should follow the following template, which is made to encourage critical analysis.

Thread title: A few words to succinctly sum up your idea. Acceptable: "Delete all pages". Unacceptable: "I had an idea…"

Thread summary: A few words to quickly sum up your suggestion (imagine you're reminding someone who's already heard it).

Thread contents:

 **Description:** A full description of your suggestion. Be detailed, but concise. **Reason:** Why are you suggesting this? **Advantages:** What benefit does this suggestion bring? **Disadvantages:** Can you think of any downsides?

Please follow this template unless you really feel that it doesn't apply to your idea, or if your idea is so simple that a template would be overkill.

item.title: Is there the possibility of an app?
item.content:encoded: Just as the title states. Is there chance there will be an SCP app better suited to mobile devices? I know it would probably a tech nightmare, but I feel it would improve mobile compatibility a metric shitload.

item.title: Will users get notifications?
item.content:encoded: I noticed a frustrating detail. I don't get notified when I got a reply in a forum and such. I can only hope the new project will have a feature like that.

item.title: Suggestion - Comment Voting
item.description: What it says on the tin
item.content:encoded: Many a time have I seen replies of "Wish I could upvote/like/updoot/vote on comments". My suggestion, the ability to like or dislike comments along with a score for each comment. While forums would automatically load sorted from oldest-newest, there would be an option to sort from highest-lowest. This would allow for people to draw more attention to comments that they believe contain good critique or important information.

item.title: https
item.content:encoded:
I'm sure a lot of the people on this project are aware already, but there's been a movement over the last few years to make sure more websites use https instead of the older http. I won't go into the details, but https is much more secure, as it is encrypted, whereas http is not.

Whatever form PF takes in the future, personally I just wanted to make sure this was a consideration (even if you won't be thinking about it for a while). Maybe you'd already thought about it, but as I pointed out in another thread, the current site does not have https, so if we are making a new site, we should really be using it.

item.title: A "nice to have"
item.description: I'll post this here before I forget about it
item.content:encoded:
A "nice to have" (but by no means essential) feature of articles on the next platform could be a section above the main article with the title of the SCP (so that you don't have to navigate back to the main list to search for it), and a link to the author's other works.
Just an idea.

item.title: Universal/Local Rating
item.content:encoded:
Footnotes
1. They are anyway, haha.
2. Although this would be up to staff of the respective branch.

I really don't know if this is the place for this. If not, Staff is free to move this1.

So, my idea is the following:

  • Original Articles of a branch (Such as an SCP-XXXX on this, the main branch, or an SCP-XXX-FR on the french branch) would have one rating module. The Universal rating module, where you can rate the content of the article.
  • Translations of Articles on a branch (such as SCP-XXXX on the portuguese branch or an SCP-XXX-FR on the International Translation Archive (which I hope will be migrated into the main branch in PF)) will have two rating modules. The Universal Content one, and a Local one with which you can rate the translation quality.
  • The Local Rating Module will not affect other local rating modules. E.g., if someone rates a +1 on the translation of SCP-XXXX on the spanish-speaking branch, the translation rating module on the german-speaking branch is unaffected.
  • The Universal Rating Modules of the same article affect each other, as the name suggests. E.g.: If someone from the russian branch rates the content of an article with a -1, this -1 is then also shown on the content rating module of all the other branches, including the main branch.
  • If the content rating module reaches the deletion threshold of the branch (-10 on the main branch), it gets deleted, as we are used to.
  • If the translation rating module of a branch reaches a threshold, it is given to the respective Review Team of the branch (if extant) and is going to be retranslated. If a branch does not have a respective Review Team, then Staff is free to give it to a voluntary. This voluntary needs to have written a translation before with a positive rating2.

Hope you like my idea!

Oh, and a question, is the development of PF gonna be open source?

item.title: Aesthetics
item.content:encoded: My request is pretty simple: don't change up the CSS theme too much. I like what we have already, I think it's both stylish & functional, and the more changes there are, the harder it'll be migrate existing CSS re-skins. This is more of a personal thing, and the public at large just might not agree with it, but I don't feel that the request is too unreasonable or controversial.
